prompt: Drag each cost to the fund it draws from. One of them should not be funded at all yet — it needs to go through change control first.
caption: Baseline · contingency (known-unknowns) · management (unknown-unknowns).

tokens.label: 'Approved dev labour'
tokens.why: 'Planned, in-scope work sits in the cost baseline — not a reserve.'

tokens.label: 'Known vendor-delay risk fund'
tokens.why: 'An identified risk (known-unknown) is funded from the contingency reserve, which the PM controls.'

tokens.label: 'Integration risk buffer'
tokens.why: 'Another identified risk → contingency reserve.'

tokens.label: 'Possible unknown new regulation'
tokens.why: 'An unforeseeable unknown-unknown → management reserve, released only with sponsor approval.'

tokens.label: 'Sponsor pet feature, no CR'
tokens.why: 'Out of scope with no change request — it belongs in no reserve. Raise a change request first.'

explanation: The cost baseline holds planned scope. The contingency reserve covers identified risks (known-unknowns) and is the PM's to spend. The management reserve covers unknown-unknowns and needs sponsor approval. Unbudgeted new scope with no CR is funded by none of them.

prompt: Retention is slipping. Drag each factor to its Herzberg category — Hygiene (its absence causes dissatisfaction) or Motivator (its presence drives satisfaction).

tokens.label: 'Below-market pay'
tokens.why: 'Salary is a hygiene factor — poor pay dissatisfies, but good pay does not itself motivate.'

tokens.label: 'Micromanagement'
tokens.why: 'Supervision and policy are hygiene — fixing them only removes dissatisfaction.'

tokens.label: 'Slow VPN / old laptop'
tokens.why: 'Working conditions are hygiene — bad tools frustrate; good tools are merely expected.'

tokens.label: 'Meaningful, high-impact work'
tokens.why: 'Meaningful achievement is a true motivator — it drives satisfaction and engagement.'

tokens.label: 'Recognition for good work'
tokens.why: 'Recognition and achievement are motivators — they lift engagement.'

tokens.label: 'Growth & advancement'
tokens.why: 'Advancement and growth are motivators — they build lasting satisfaction.'

explanation: Hygiene factors (pay, policies, supervision, conditions) only prevent dissatisfaction — fixing them will not motivate. Motivators (achievement, recognition, meaningful work, growth) are what actually raise satisfaction, so a retention fix must add motivators, not just patch hygiene.

prompt: A 30% budget cut with a fixed release date. Drag each backlog item to its MoSCoW priority for THIS release — only Must-haves are guaranteed to ship.

tokens.label: 'Regulatory audit log'
tokens.why: 'Compliance is non-negotiable — it ships regardless of the cut.'

tokens.label: 'SSO / login security'
tokens.why: 'A security baseline is a Must — the release is unsafe without it.'

tokens.label: 'PDF export'
tokens.why: 'Valuable and expected, but the release still works if it slips one iteration → Should.'

tokens.label: 'In-app onboarding tour'
tokens.why: 'Nice polish that helps adoption but is not essential → Could, if time allows.'

tokens.label: 'Custom colour themes'
tokens.why: 'A pure delighter — first to drop under a budget cut → Won’t (this release).'

explanation: Under a cut, Must = non-negotiable (compliance, security baseline); Should = important but deferrable without breaking the release; Could = nice-to-have if capacity remains; Won’t = explicitly out of scope this release. Cosmetic delighters go first; compliance and security never move off Must.

prompt: Map the 'invoice approval' process. Drag each element to its SIPOC lane. One item is outside this process boundary.

tokens.label: 'AP clerk'
tokens.why: 'The clerk provides the input into the process — a Supplier.'

tokens.label: 'Scanned invoice'
tokens.why: 'The invoice is the Input the process acts on.'

tokens.label: 'Manager approves & codes'
tokens.why: 'Approval and coding is the transforming activity — the Process.'

tokens.label: 'Posted ledger entry'
tokens.why: 'The posted entry is the Output the process produces.'

tokens.label: 'Treasury team'
tokens.why: 'Treasury receives and uses the output — the Customer.'

tokens.label: 'ERP vendor contract'
tokens.why: 'The ERP contract is outside this process boundary — it belongs to procurement, not the invoice-approval SIPOC.'

explanation: SIPOC bounds a process: Suppliers provide Inputs, the Process transforms them into Outputs for Customers. The ERP vendor contract sits outside this boundary — a common trap that pulls in unrelated context.

prompt: Drag each communication method onto the situation it best fits.
caption: Push · Pull · Interactive.

tokens.label: 'Push'
tokens.why: 'One-way distribution to many with no confirmation needed → push (email, memo, broadcast).'

tokens.label: 'Pull'
tokens.why: 'A large audience retrieves information at its own discretion → pull (portal, dashboard, wiki).'

tokens.label: 'Interactive'
tokens.why: 'Real-time, multi-directional exchange for negotiation or conflict → interactive (meeting, call).'

explanation: Push = sender distributes and cannot confirm understanding (memos, status emails). Pull = recipients access at will, good for large volumes (dashboards, repositories). Interactive = real-time two-way, essential for negotiation, conflict, and building agreement.

prompt: Configuration audit before release. The baseline is locked at v{{V}}. Tap every artifact whose version does not match the baseline — it was changed without an approved change request.

items.label: names[i]
items.why: isBad[i] ? (names[i] + ' is at v' + (V+1) + ' — off the locked v' + V + ' baseline, with no approved CR.') : (names[i] + ' is at v' + V + ' — matches the baseline.')

explanation: Under configuration management every baselined artifact stays at the baseline version until a change request is approved. Any artifact showing a different version was changed outside change control and breaks baseline integrity.

prompt: Weighted source selection — Technical 40%, Cost 35%, Schedule 25%. The per-criterion scores are shown; the weighted totals are not. Tap the vendor with the highest weighted score.

items.label: 'Vendor ' + names[i]
items.why: 'Weighted score ' + round1(tot[i]/100) + (i == win ? ' — the highest, so best value.' : ' — not the highest.')

explanation: Weighted score = 0.40·Technical + 0.35·Cost + 0.25·Schedule → A {{round1(tot[0]/100)}}, B {{round1(tot[1]/100)}}, C {{round1(tot[2]/100)}}. Vendor {{names[win]}} wins. Note the vendor with the best Cost score (Vendor {{names[cheap]}}) does NOT win — lowest price is not best value.

prompt: SAFe WSJF = Cost of Delay ÷ Job Size. Each story shows its CoD and Size — not its WSJF. Tap the story the team should pull in first.

items.label: 'Story ' + names[i]
items.why: 'WSJF = ' + cod[i] + '/' + size[i] + ' = ' + round1(cod[i]/size[i]) + (i == win ? ' — the highest, pull it first.' : ' — not the highest.')

explanation: WSJF pulls the highest Cost-of-Delay-per-size job first, so small urgent work beats big high-value work. Here Story {{names[win]}} has the top ratio — note the story with the highest Cost of Delay alone is NOT the winner, because its size drags the ratio down.

prompt: Each risk was mitigated — its score dropped from initial to residual. The escalation threshold is 12. Tap every risk whose RESIDUAL score still breaches it (≥ 12) and needs a contingency plan.
guard: count([max(1,init[0]-drop[0])>=12, max(1,init[1]-drop[1])>=12, max(1,init[2]-drop[2])>=12, max(1,init[3]-drop[3])>=12]) >= 1 && count([max(1,init[0]-drop[0])>=12, max(1,init[1]-drop[1])>=12, max(1,init[2]-drop[2])>=12, max(1,init[3]-drop[3])>=12]) <= 3

items.label: 'R' + (i+1)
items.why: resid[i] >= 12 ? ('Residual ' + resid[i] + ' ≥ 12 — mitigation left it above threshold; it still needs a contingency plan.') : ('Residual ' + resid[i] + ' < 12 — mitigation brought it under threshold; no further action.')

explanation: Residual risk is what remains AFTER a response. Only risks whose residual score still meets the threshold ({{nOver}} here) need further action — a high initial score that was mitigated below 12 does not, and a small residual is accepted.

prompt: You are in Month 2 of an 18-month programme. Rolling-wave planning details only the next 1–2 phases and keeps distant phases high-level. Tap the phase that has been decomposed too early — task-level detail this far out is false precision.

items.label: 'Phase ' + (i+1)
items.why: i == bad ? ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is ' + timing[i] + ' out but broken to task level — premature detail / false precision.') : (i <= 1 ? ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is near-term (' + timing[i] + ') — detailing it now is correct.') : ('Phase ' + (i+1) + ' is ' + timing[i] + ' out and kept high-level — correct for rolling-wave.'))

explanation: Rolling-wave / progressive elaboration details near-term work and leaves distant work high-level until it approaches. Task-level detail on Phase {{bad+1}} ({{timing[bad]}} out) is false precision — it will be reworked long before the phase begins.

prompt: Every point is within the control limits. Tap the run of seven consecutive points on the same side of the mean — the 'rule of seven' that signals non-random variation and warrants investigation.

items.label: 'point ' + (i+1)
items.why: (i >= runStart && i < runStart+7) ? 'One of seven consecutive points on the same side of the mean — the rule-of-seven signal.' : 'Sits on its normal side of the mean — not part of the seven-in-a-row.'

explanation: A process can be inside its control limits yet still out of control: seven consecutive points on one side of the mean (the rule of seven / run rule) is a non-random pattern that warrants investigation, even with no limit breach.

prompt: A change request has been raised (first box, done). Drag the next three steps into the boxes in the order they must happen. Two of the options do not belong.

tokens.label: 'Assess impact'
tokens.why: 'First analyse the impact on scope, cost, schedule, risk and quality.'

tokens.label: 'CCB decision'
tokens.why: 'The change control board approves, defers or rejects it — the control gate.'

tokens.label: 'Update baselines'
tokens.why: 'Only an approved change updates the baselines and plans, then it is communicated.'

tokens.label: 'Implement immediately'
tokens.why: 'Acting before the CCB decides bypasses integrated change control.'

tokens.label: 'Notify sponsor only'
tokens.why: 'A courtesy note is not a decision — it skips impact analysis and the CCB.'

explanation: Integrated change control: assess the impact, take it to the CCB for a decision, then (if approved) update the baselines and communicate. Implementing first, or only notifying the sponsor, skips the control step.

prompt: Value stream — the boxes are value-add steps; the number in each gap is the days work sits in a queue between them. Tap the queue where inventory waits longest — the bottleneck to attack first.
guard: waits[0]!=waits[1] && waits[0]!=waits[2] && waits[0]!=waits[3] && waits[1]!=waits[2] && waits[1]!=waits[3] && waits[2]!=waits[3] && (argmax(waits)==0 || waits[argmax(waits)] > waits[argmax(waits)-1])

items.label: 'queue ' + (i+1)
items.why: i == win ? (waits[i] + ' days — the longest queue; this bottleneck dominates lead time.') : (waits[i] + ' days — a shorter queue, not the main constraint.')

explanation: Lead time is dominated by waiting, not value-add work. Attack the longest queue (the bottleneck) first — speeding up a non-bottleneck step just makes its queue grow.

prompt: {{ mode==0 ? 'Cost overruns are running at a typical rate and are expected to continue. Tap the EAC formula to forecast with.' : (mode==1 ? 'The overrun was a one-off; the remaining work will run at the planned rate. Tap the EAC formula to forecast with.' : 'The original estimate is no longer valid and both cost and schedule pressure will persist. Tap the EAC formula to forecast with.') }}

items.label: f[i]
items.why: i == mode ? 'Correct — this assumption matches the scenario.' : 'This formula assumes a different future than the scenario describes.'

explanation: {{ mode==0 ? 'When current cost performance is typical and will continue, EAC = BAC/CPI extends the trend.' : (mode==1 ? 'When the overrun was a one-off and remaining work runs to plan, EAC = AC + (BAC-EV) adds the remaining budget at value.' : 'When both cost and schedule variances will persist, EAC = AC + (BAC-EV)/(CPI×SPI) deflates the remaining work by both indices.') }}

prompt: Activity {{names[badLink]}} must finish, then a mandatory {{lag}}-day wait passes before {{names[badLink+1]}} may start. The links are drawn finish-to-start with no lag. Tap the dependency link that must become FS + {{lag}}.

items.label: names[i] + '→' + names[i+1]
items.why: i == badLink ? ('This ' + names[i] + '→' + names[i+1] + ' link must become FS + ' + lag + ' to model the mandatory wait.') : ('The ' + names[i] + '→' + names[i+1] + ' link is correct as FS + 0.')

explanation: A mandatory delay between two activities is modelled as a lag on the dependency (FS + {{lag}}) — not as extra duration on either bar and not as a new activity. Only the {{names[badLink]}}→{{names[badLink+1]}} link needs the lag.

prompt: Drag each feature to its Kano category — Must-be (expected; its absence dissatisfies), Performance (more is better), or Delighter (unexpected; its presence delights).

tokens.label: 'Data encryption'
tokens.why: 'Security is a basic expectation — its absence causes strong dissatisfaction, its presence is merely assumed.'

tokens.label: 'SSO login'
tokens.why: 'Expected baseline functionality — a Must-be, not a differentiator.'

tokens.label: 'Report export speed'
tokens.why: 'Linear satisfaction — faster is better, slower is worse: a Performance attribute.'

tokens.label: 'Search relevance'
tokens.why: 'More of it steadily raises satisfaction — a Performance attribute.'

tokens.label: 'Dark mode theme'
tokens.why: 'Unexpected polish — its presence delights, its absence is not missed: a Delighter.'

tokens.label: 'AI auto-summary'
tokens.why: 'A surprising extra that excites users — a Delighter (until it becomes expected).'

explanation: Kano sorts features by how they drive satisfaction: Must-be (expected — only dissatisfies if missing), Performance (linear — more is better), and Delighters (unexpected — delight when present). Delighters decay into expectations over time, so the baseline must always cover the Must-bes first.

prompt: At 60% complete the sponsor will not increase the budget. Read the TCPI-to-budget shown. What does it mean for the remaining work?

choices.text: 'The remaining work must be done about ' + pct + '% more cost-efficiently than it has been so far.'
'The project is ' + pct + '% over budget overall.'
'You need ' + pct + '% more funding to finish.'
'Cost performance will recover on its own.'

explanation: TCPI is the cost efficiency REQUIRED on the remaining work to still hit the target. A TCPI above 1.0 means the team must perform better than it has — here about {{pct}}% more efficiently — which is a warning that the target may be unrealistic, not a measure of current overrun or a funding request.

prompt: A 2-week sprint is mid-flight when a phase gate arrives. Tap the ceremony that must NOT be skipped — the one that produces the reviewed increment the gate's go/no-go decision depends on.

items.label: names[slot[i]]
items.why: slot[i] == 1 ? 'The sprint review demonstrates the increment to stakeholders — that reviewed increment feeds the gate decision, so it cannot be skipped.' : (slot[i] == 0 ? 'The daily standup is routine coordination — useful, but not what the gate needs.' : (slot[i] == 2 ? 'The retro improves the team, but it is internal and not gate-critical.' : 'An informal chat is no substitute for a formal reviewed increment.'))

explanation: In a hybrid stage-gate + agile model, the gate's go/no-go needs a reviewed, demonstrable increment. That is exactly what the sprint review produces, so it is the one ceremony you protect when the gate lands mid-sprint — standups, retros and informal chats do not feed the gate decision.

prompt: Sprint burn-up — the grey line is total scope, the solid line is completed work. The change log shows only ONE approved CR (marked ✓). Tap the point where scope was added with no matching change request.

items.label: 'Day ' + (i+1)
items.why: i == badDay ? 'Scope jumped here with no matching CR — unauthorized scope injected outside change control.' : (i == legitDay ? 'Scope rose here too, but this is the approved CR (✓) — legitimate.' : 'No scope change on this day.')

explanation: A burn-up chart exposes scope changes as upward steps in the scope line. The step on day {{legitDay+1}} matches an approved CR; the step on day {{badDay+1}} has none — scope added outside integrated change control, which is what the audit must catch.

prompt: The project is at concept stage — no feasibility study yet. Tap the estimate range that honestly reflects the uncertainty at this early point on the cone.

items.label: opts[perm[i]]
items.why: perm[i] == 0 ? 'Correct — early estimates are order-of-magnitude; a wide range is honest at concept.' : (perm[i] == 1 ? 'False precision — you cannot be ±10% before a feasibility study.' : 'That is execution-level precision; refusing to estimate at all is not the ask.')

explanation: The cone of uncertainty is widest at the start and narrows as the project progresses. At concept a rough order-of-magnitude estimate (a wide range, e.g. ±50%) is appropriate and honest; a tight range this early is false precision.
